questions.question: Show that Z7* = {1,2,3,4,5,6} forms a group under multiplication modulo 7. Find its order and list all elements that are generators.
keyPoints: Define closure: product of any two non-zero residues mod 7 is non-zero
For each element, find multiplicative inverse mod 7
Generators are elements of order 6: 3 and 5

questions.question: Define permutation group Sn. If σ = (1 2 3)(4 5) in S5, find its order and determine whether it is even or odd.
keyPoints: Sn is set of all permutations of {1,2,...,n} with n! elements
Write σ as product of cycles: one 3-cycle and one 2-cycle
Even × odd = odd, so σ is odd permutation

questions.question: State and prove Euler's Theorem. Hence compute 3^100 mod 11.
keyPoints: State Euler's Theorem: a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) for gcd(a,n)=1

questions.question: Define the alternating group An. Find the number of elements in A4 and list all its elements.
keyPoints: An is subgroup of Sn containing all even permutations
Elements of A4: identity (1), 3-cycles: (1 2 3), (1 3 2), (1 2 4), (1 4 2), (1 3 4), (1 4 3), (2 3 4), (2 4 3), products of two disjoint transpositions: (1 2)(3 4), (1 3)(2 4), (1 4)(2 3)

questions.question: State Cayley's Theorem. Give an example of a group of order 6 represented as a permutation group.
keyPoints: Cayley's Theorem: Every finite group is isomorphic to a subgroup of Sn for some n
Consider cyclic group C6 of order 6
It is isomorphic to subgroup of S6
Elements of C6: {e, a, a², a³, a⁴, a⁵} can be represented as permutations on {e, a, a², a³, a⁴, a⁵}
Multiplication by a permutes the elements: a^k → a^(k+1) mod 6
This gives subgroup of S6 isomorphic to C6
Alternative: S3 itself is a permutation group of order 6

questions.question: Find the multiplicative inverse of 5 modulo 17 using Euler's Theorem or otherwise.
Express the permutation σ = (1 4 7)(2 5)(3 6 8 9) in S9 as product of disjoint cycles and find its order.
keyPoints: Given permutation already in cycle notation
Three disjoint cycles: (1 4 7) length 3, (2 5) length 2, (3 6 8 9) length 4
Cycle decomposition: already disjoint, so same as given

questions.question: Prove that Zp* is a cyclic group for any prime p. Find all generators of Z11*.
keyPoints: Theorem: For prime p, multiplicative group Zp* is cyclic
Proof: Zp* has order p-1, let d divide p-1
Number of elements of order d in Zp* is ≤ φ(d)
Sum of elements of all orders equals p-1
This forces equality, proving existence of element of order p-1
To find generators of Z11*: p-1 = 10, divisors: 1,2,5,10

questions.question: (a) Define permutation, symmetric group Sn, and alternating group An. (b) Prove that An is a subgroup of Sn and has order n!/2. (c) Show that (1 2 3) is even and (1 2) is odd in S3.
keyPoints: (a) Permutation: bijective mapping from {1,...,n} to itself. Sn: set of all n! permutations. An: set of all even permutations in Sn

(b) Proof: An is non-empty (contains identity), closed under composition (product of even permutations is even), and each element has inverse (inverse of even is even). Order: half of |Sn| = n!/2 because exactly half permutations are even
(c) (1 2 3) = (1 3)(1 2) = product of 2 transpositions, so even. (1 2) = single transposition, so odd. Verify using sign function: sign(σ) = (-1)^k where k is number of transpositions

questions.question: (a) State and prove Lagrange's Theorem. (b) Using Lagrange's Theorem, prove that in a group of order p (prime), every non-identity element has order p. (c) Hence show that any group of prime order is cyclic.
keyPoints: (a) Lagrange's Theorem: Order of subgroup divides order of finite group G. Proof: Left cosets partition G, each coset has same size as subgroup, so |G| = |H| × [G:H]
(b) Let G have prime order p. Take any non-identity element a. Order of a divides p by Lagrange's Theorem. Since a ≠ e, order > 1, so order must be p
(c) Since a has order p = |G|, ⟨a⟩ = G. Thus G is cyclic generated by a
Application to Zp*: Every element has order dividing p-1, generators have order p-1

questions.question: Let σ = (1 2 3 4 5) and τ = (1 3)(2 4) be permutations in S5. Compute: (a) σ², σ⁻¹, τ² (b) στ and τσ (c) Determine order and parity of σ, τ, στ.
keyPoints: (a) σ² = (1 3 5)(2 4); σ⁴ = σ⁻¹ = (1 5 4 3 2); τ² = identity e
(c) σ is 5-cycle, order = 5 (odd permutation). τ is product of 2 transpositions, order = 2 (even). στ: find cycle decomposition, determine order by LCM, parity by sign
